lifeNote: The things that matter most rarely announce themselves. They just show up and wait to see if you're paying attention.
headline: The Bypass That Wasn't
tldr: March CPI hit +0.9% MoM — the largest monthly surge since 2022, driven by a 10.9% energy shock — but the YoY headline of 3.3% landed below the feared 3.7%, and core held at 2.6%, a tenth below forecast. Rate cut odds surged to 85% for June. Then Iran hit Saudi Arabia's East-West pipeline, the very infrastructure designed to bypass the Hormuz chokepoint, while the Strait itself remains effectively closed. Both the primary route and the contingency are now compromised.

take.title: The Day the Bypass Broke: Why Iran's Saudi Strikes Changed the Energy Map More Than the Ceasefire
take.preview: For three days, the market has been trading a ceasefire narrative. Oil dropped 17% on Day 1. Equities rallied 2.5%. The VIX fell below its pre-war level. The story was simple: war paused, risk off, back to normal. Then Iran hit the one piece of infrastructure that made "back to normal" physically possible.

innerGame.quote: It is the mark of an educated mind to be able to entertain a thought without accepting it.
innerGame.action: Identify one belief you hold about your work, your market thesis, your relationships, or yourself that you haven't questioned in at least a month. State it plainly. Then spend five minutes actively building the case against it. Not to destroy it. To test whether it's load-bearing or decorative. If the belief survives the challenge, it comes back stronger. If it doesn't survive, you just saved yourself from building on a cracked foundation.

model.name: Language as Thought Constraint: The Sapir-Whorf Hypothesis and Why the Words You Use Determine What You Can See
model.preview: "Cooler than feared." That was the universal framing of this morning's CPI print. The monthly number hit +0.9%, matching the consensus fear — the largest monthly surge since 2022. But the annual headline of 3.3% landed below the feared 3.7%, and that's the number the market chose to celebrate. Rate cut odds surged. Equities rallied. The language of the reaction shaped the market's behavior more than the data itself. But step back from the framing: annual inflation is 3.3%, its highest since May 2024, driven almost entirely by a 10.9% energy surge, and the monthly print was a genuine shock at +0.9%. Core at 2.6% actually came in below forecast. The market had two numbers to choose from — the monthly monster and the annual beat — and the language picked the one that fit the relief narrative. The frame "cooler than feared" erased the structural question entirely. The language determined the perception, not the other way around.
